Really? Good for you. The majority of people don't usually stick with their childhood dreams. Whether or not you're an engineer, executive, accountant, salesperson, etc., you want to believe you're capable of achieving the extraordinary. As children, our daydreams feature ourselves as primary players in great moments. An actor, winning an Oscar. An athlete, hitting the game winning shot. As you grew older, however, external influences, people who were around you, talked more of what you can't or shouldn't do than what was possible. So, as a result, your hopes and dreams get pushed back and even obscured. But, when something you're involved in rekindles that passion and conveys a path of how to get there, we allow our perceptions of what's possible to change. Never underestimate the influence power of hopes and dreams. Now, what if they're crazy? What if what they're saying makes no sense? When you're trying to figure out how your counterpart views the world, you've got to be mindful not to fall into their crazy trap. This is critical when uncovering black swans. Like we stated in a previous chapter, human nature initially triggers us to reject the unknown. When we get confronted by it, it scares us and we ignore it, run away from it, or label it in a way that allows us to dismiss it. In the negotiation world, this usually takes the form of calling the other party crazy. When you're almost ready to throw up your hands and declare they're crazy, it's often the best moment for discovering black swans. It's when we hear or see something that doesn't make sense, something crazy, that a crucial fork on the road is presented. Push forward into what you initially can't process. There's two critical go-to moves here which can bail you out in these types of moments. First, the mirror. Just like I did during the Chase Manhattan Bank robbery, where I mirrored the critical words the hostage taker had just said, I not only bought myself a crucial few moments to think, he spilled vital information as an involuntary response. The second go-to move you can always implore here, a simple label. It sounds like you've got a good reason for saying that, or it sounds like you've given this a lot of thought. Either of these will simultaneously provide you both valuable information and time to process what you've heard. Harvard professors Deepak Mahaultra and Max Bazerman once wrote a book called Negotiation Genius, in which they highlight several common reasons you might mistakenly call your counterparts crazy. I like to go over these quickly. Mistake number one, they're ill-informed. Very often, the other side is acting on bad information, and when people have bad information, they make bad choices. When someone is operating within complete information, they appear crazy to those who have different or more complete information. When you're faced with someone like this in a negotiation, your job is to discover what they don't know, and then guide them to that information. Mistake number two, they're constrained. In any negotiation where your counterpart is acting wobbly, there exists a distinct possibility there are things they can't do. And quite often, they aren't eager to reveal these things based on their fears. Such constraints can make the same as counterparts seem irrational. The other side might not be able to do something because of legal advice or because of promises already made, or even to avoid setting a precedent. Or they simply might not have the power to close a deal. Mistake number three, they have other interests. Hidden interests are common. You're better off expecting them to be there. Your counterpart will often reject offers for reasons that have nothing to do with the merits. Examples of this could be a client may put off buying your product so that their calendar year closes before the invoice hits, thus increasing his chance for a promotion. An employee might quit in the middle of a career making project just before bonus season because he or she has learned that colleagues are making more money. Whatever the specifics of the situation, these people aren't acting irrationally. They're simply complying with needs and desires that you don't yet understand. Your job is to bring these Black swans to light. When they seem crazy in their written communications, think about meeting in person. We communicate these days so much through email and text that much of that meeting is lost in translation. It's obvious there's no context of nonverbal cues and tone of voice available. Furthermore, when you actually do get FaceTime, formal business meetings, structured encounters and planned negotiation sessions are often the least revealing kinds of FaceTime depending on where your focus is. You and your team will generally be focused on their primary speaker, their lead, if you will. The most on-guarded reactions will be on their supporting players, those who don't feel the glare of attention on them, pay special attention to anything that interrupts the flow of their communication, simply noting their body language after your questions, how others respond verbally and nonverbally, can reveal a goldmine. Crazy, like leverage, is in the eye of the beholder. To be seemingly irrational doesn't mean someone isn't acting in a pattern. Figuring out that pattern can be crucial in unearthing your counterparts' religion. Then you can frame your objectives in ways that will resonate with them and give you your best chances for successful outcomes.
